EGYPTIAN AIRPORTS COMPANY (EAC)

Supply, Installation, Operation & Maintenance of

Special Airports Integrated Systems

VOLUME 2

TECHNICAL PROPOSAL

AODB · FIDS · RMS · CUPPS · LDCS · BRS · APOC · Baggage Tracking

Sharm El-Sheikh · Hurghada · Alexandria · Aswan · Abu Simbel · Assiut · Sohag · Luxor · EAC COC/CCC

Submitted by

RESA  —  WAISL

Airport Operational & Common-Use Systems by RESA (Groupe ADP)

Systems Integration, APOC Platform & Managed Services by WAISL

Commercial-in-Confidence  ·  June 2026

Table of Contents

# 1.  Executive Summary

The Egyptian Airports Company (EAC) is modernising the operational technology backbone of eight of its airports — Sharm El-Sheikh (T1 & T2), Hurghada (T1 & T2), Alexandria (T1 & T2), Aswan, Abu Simbel, Assiut, Sohag and Luxor — together with the central Operations Control Centre (COC/CCC). The programme replaces and operates the airports' core passenger-processing and flight-management systems (CUPPS, LDCS, AODB, RMS, FIDS, BRS), introduces a new Airport Predictive Operations Command Centre (APOC) and a centralised baggage-tracking capability, and wraps the whole estate in a standardised, highly-available infrastructure with a five-year SLA.

This Volume 2 is the consolidated technical response to the full Request for Proposal. It is submitted by the RESA–WAISL partnership: the passenger-processing and airport-operational systems are delivered on the RESA product suite — a field-proven, COTS, IATA-aligned family of airport applications installed at some 300 airports worldwide — while WAISL acts as the systems integrator and operator and delivers the APOC intelligence layer on its own AIOP (Airport Intelligence Operational Platform). RESA is the system owner providing the licensed products and five-year technical support; WAISL operates the programme as the single point of accountability to EAC.

Our central proposition is simple: EAC should buy proven, standardised products operated as one estate, not a collection of bespoke point-solutions. Every airport receives the same RESA application stack and the same infrastructure pattern, so a technician trained at Sharm El-Sheikh can support Aswan, a template built once is deployed everywhere, and the COC/CCC sees all eight airports through a single pane of glass while each site keeps full local autonomy if the network is lost.

## 1.1  Understanding of EAC's Requirement

EAC's network is dominated by leisure and charter traffic concentrated at the Red Sea and Nile-valley gateways, with pronounced seasonal and daily peaks. The RFP therefore mandates a stand-alone topology — production servers physically located at each airport so operations never depend on a wide-area link — combined with near-real-time replication to COC/CCC for cross-airport coordination (diversions, network-wide movements) and centralised supervision. It demands full IATA/ICAO standards compliance, fully-redundant architectures with no single point of failure, a two-year hardware/software warranty after Global Provisional Acceptance (GPA), and a five-year SLA with on-site staffing at every airport. These constraints shape every design decision in this proposal.

## 1.2  The RESA–WAISL Solution in One Page

The solution is an integrated, on-premises airport-systems estate in which each functional system is a configuration of a mature RESA product, bound together by the RESA FAIRWAY integration bus and consolidated for command-and-control by WAISL's APOC platform:

| EAC System | Delivered by (RESA / WAISL product) | Role in the estate |
| --- | --- | --- |
| CUPPS — Common Use Passenger Processing | RESA CREWS (+ SKYCHECK CUSS, SKYDROP SBD, LAYER ONE) | Shared check-in & boarding for all airlines; CUPPS RP 1797 certified, CUTE backward-compatible. |
| LDCS — Local Departure Control | RESA ON BOARD (+ Evionica Weight & Balance) | Full check-in, boarding and load control for airlines and ground handlers. |
| AODB — Airport Operational Database | RESA INFOPAX (FIMS) | Single source of flight truth; AFTN/Type-B, SSIM, A-CDM; feeds every other system. |
| RMS — Resource Management | RESA INFOPAX-RMS | Gantt-based allocation of stands, gates, counters and belts with what-if and IROPS. |
| FIDS — Flight Information Display | RESA VISTA | Multilingual passenger and staff displays, rule-driven, with emergency override. |
| BRS — Baggage Reconciliation | RESA BAGERA (+ BAGWAY MDS) | Resolution 753 real-time reconciliation and tracking on rugged HHTs. |
| APOC — Predictive Ops Command Centre | WAISL AIOP / APOC (+ RESA FAIRWAY DX) | Intelligence layer above all systems: real-time picture, prediction, what-if, KPIs. |
| Baggage Tracking & Lost-and-Found | RESA PAXTRACK | Passenger-facing tracking, chain-of-custody and lost-&-found lifecycle. |
| Integration & Infrastructure | RESA FAIRWAY + WAISL on-prem HA infrastructure | Hub-and-spoke bus; dual core rooms, clustered HA, CrowdStrike EDR, COC/CCC monitoring. |

## 1.3  Key Differentiators

| Differentiator | RESA–WAISL approach | Why it matters to EAC |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Proven COTS products | Every system is a configured RESA product already live at international airports — not a bespoke build. | Lower delivery risk, faster commissioning within the 4–6 month window, fewer defects. |
| One standardised estate | Identical application stack and infrastructure pattern across all eight airports. | Train once, support anywhere; simpler maintenance; consistent resilience; easy expansion. |
| Stand-alone + central | Local servers keep each airport running if the WAN drops; INFOPAX replicates to COC/CCC. | Zero-downtime operations and true network-wide situational awareness. |
| Single accountability | WAISL owns delivery, integration and the five-year SLA; RESA underwrites the products for five years. | One throat to choke; EAC is never caught between vendors. |
| Intelligence on top | WAISL APOC adds prediction, what-if and KPI command-and-control above the RESA suite. | EAC moves from reacting to delays to anticipating them, a genuine data-driven operation. |
| Sovereignty & openness | 100% on-premises on EAC-owned hardware; open, standards-based interfaces; EAC owns the data and admin rights. | No lock-in; full data ownership; future biometric/Bluetooth upgrade path preserved. |

# 2.  About the Bidder

This proposal is submitted by the RESA–WAISL partnership. RESA — the airport-systems product owner — provides the licensed application suite and five-year product support; WAISL is the systems integrator and operator delivering the EAC programme on the RESA suite, together with WAISL’s own APOC intelligence platform.

## 2.1  RESA — Airport Systems Product Owner

The passenger-processing and airport-operational systems are built on the RESA product suite. RESA is a French airport-software house (part of Groupe ADP, the Paris airports group) with a long track record of common-use and airport-operational deployments worldwide. RESA's products are COTS, modular and IATA/ICAO-aligned, and are deployed across airports of all sizes. For this programme RESA provides the licensed products, product engineering and a five-year technical-support commitment from initial acceptance, satisfying the RFP's requirement (Section 4) that where a third-party system owner is involved, a letter of authorisation and a five-year support commitment are provided. The RESA products mapped to the EAC scope are:

| RESA / partner product | Function |
| --- | --- |
| CREWS | CUPPS common-use passenger processing platform (RP 1797 / 1.03 certified, CUTE backward-compatible). |
| ON BOARD | Local Departure Control System (check-in, boarding, seat management, APIS). |
| Evionica Weight & Balance | Load-control / weight-and-balance SaaS integrated with ON BOARD. |
| INFOPAX (FIMS) | Airport Operational Database, flight information management, A-CDM, multi-airport. |
| INFOPAX-RMS | Resource Management System — stands, gates, counters, belts, Gantt allocation. |
| VISTA | Flight Information Display System (display, rules, broadcast, content design). |
| BAGERA | Baggage Reconciliation System (IATA 753) with handheld scanning and supervision. |
| BAGWAY | Baggage messaging hub / MDS for BSM/BPM distribution between airlines and the airport. |
| PAXTRACK | Passenger and baggage tracking, lost-&-found, chain-of-custody, biometric-ready. |
| SKYCHECK / SKYDROP / LAYER ONE | CUSS self-service kiosks, self bag-drop, and self-boarding application suite. |
| FAIRWAY | Communication hub / enterprise service bus connecting all systems and external feeds. |
| CLEVER | Business-intelligence and reporting layer (incl. Power BI integration). |

RESA Airport Data Systems is a French airport-software house with over 30 years' specialisation in airport operational IT. RESA solutions are installed in approximately 300 airports worldwide, processing more than 500 million passengers informed, checked-in and boarded through some 15,000 display units and workstations used by over 450 airlines — directly relevant scale and maturity for EAC's eight-airport, centrally-supervised network.

Representative multi-airport programmes delivered by RESA include:

| Programme / Operator | RESA systems deployed | Scale |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Paris CDG & Orly (Aéroports de Paris) | CUPPS, FIDS, BRS, LDCS, Self-Boarding Gates, MDS, Passenger Tracking | World's largest common-use platform — 2,000+ workstations, 170+ self-boarding gates, ~300 BRS scanners, 5,000+ FIDS terminals |
| 7 airports, Pakistan (PCAA) | CUPPS, LDCS, CUSS, BRS, Passenger Tracking | 22M pax/yr; 500+ CUPPS workstations, 100+ BRS scanners |
| 5 international airports, Nigeria (FAAN) | CUPPS, LDCS, CUSS, SBG, BRS, AODB, RMS, FIDS, Billing, BI | 13M+ pax/yr; 245 CUPPS, 30 CUSS, 300+ FIDS terminals |
| 14 regional airports, Greece (Fraport) | BRS, Message Distribution System | Standardised regional network |
| Multi-airport, Thailand (Dept. of Airports) | CUPPS, CUSS, Self-Bag-Drop | Multi-site common-use deployment |

RESA is an IATA Strategic Partner since 2006, was the first airport-IT supplier to obtain CUPPS certification (2009), leads the IATA CUPPS and Common-Use working groups, follows the ACI World ACRIS group, and obtained EUROCONTROL A-CDM certification in 2018. RESA's cybersecurity posture is governed by an ISMS aligned with ISO/IEC 27001:2022, with practices aligned to the EU NIS 2 Directive, the French Military Programming Law (MPL) and GDPR — consistent with EAC ICT, ECAA and Egyptian NCSA NIS requirements.

## 2.2  WAISL — Integrator and Managed-Service Provider

WAISL is a specialist airport-technology and managed-services organisation that works within the airport sector. That focus means WAISL's delivery and support resources are dedicated to running live airport operations rather than divided across unrelated industries. At the centre of WAISL's offer is AIOP (Airport Intelligence Operational Platform), a unified operational layer that integrates the systems running across an airport into a single real-time picture and adds predictive command-and-control on top. AIOP is in production at major international airports including Delhi (DIAL) and Hyderabad (GHIAL), and the APOC platform offered to EAC is a configuration of this proven product, not a first-of-type build. WAISL's delivery model is built around a single point of accountability: EAC interacts with WAISL, and WAISL manages everything beneath it, including all subcontracted and product scope.

## 2.3  Delivery Model — Single Point of Accountability

WAISL is the prime point of contact for EAC throughout supply, installation, integration, commissioning, training and the five-year SLA. RESA delivers and underwrites the products; WAISL integrates them into EAC's environment, operates them under the SLA and is accountable for the OAT, SPA and GPA milestones. This structure gives EAC one accountable party for the whole estate while ensuring that each product is supported by its specialist manufacturer.

## 2.4  Eligibility and Track Record (Section 4)

The combined RESA–WAISL team meets the RFP eligibility conditions: specialist airport-systems experience in international airports across exactly the disciplines in scope (AODB, FIDS, RMS, CUPPS, LDCS, BRS); reference deployments exceeding 30 million annual passengers and more than 400 check-in counters per project (through WAISL's Delhi and Hyderabad operations and RESA's global common-use installed base); a biometric/Bluetooth-ready upgrade path across the suite; and the management-system certifications (ISO 9001, ISO 27001 information security, ISO 20000 IT service management and a CMMI-appraised delivery capability) expected of a programme of this scale. Detailed reference letters and certificates are provided in the commercial volume.

## 2.5  Roles & Responsibilities (Scope-Split) Matrix

WAISL is the prime contractor and the single point of accountability to EAC for the entire scope of supply, deployment, operations and support. Delivery is organised across WAISL’s integrated delivery team under a single contract; EAC contracts with, is invoiced by, and is supported by WAISL only. The matrix below sets out responsibility for each major work package across the lifecycle.

The scope-split below summarises the lead and supporting party for each major work package. RESA is the airport-systems product owner; WAISL is the systems integrator, APOC provider and operator; in-country operations are delivered by the RESA–WAISL in-country team.

## 3.4  Assumptions and Dependencies

- EAC provides obstacle-free site hand-over per airport so that the 2-month per-site installation window can start; civil, power and air-conditioning readiness in the core rooms is an EAC responsibility.

- EAC provides the training rooms; WAISL provides all other training requirements (Section 20).

- Airlines and their DCS providers participate in CUPPS/LDCS certification; new airline integration is performed jointly with the airline per IATA practice.

- Existing LAN active/passive assets are surveyed during the mandatory site survey; any reuse is confirmed after survey, otherwise new equipment is supplied.

- External feeds (AFTN/Type-B circuits, Timatic, slot/SSIM sources, EAC web, billing/ERP, master clock, PA, VDGS) are made available by EAC/authorities for interfacing.

- Where the RFP references future scope (biometrics, Bluetooth, RFID printing, self-service expansion), the delivered systems are made ready but the future scope itself is outside the current supply.

## 4.  Solution Architecture

## Principles

- Productised, not bespoke. Each system is a configuration of a proven RESA COTS product, minimising development and de-risking the 4–6 month delivery.

- Standardised across airports. One application stack and one infrastructure pattern at every site — uniform operations, support and expansion.

- Resilient by design. Fully-redundant, clustered architectures with automatic failover and no single point of failure, meeting the Section 9 HA mandate.

- Local autonomy, central oversight. Stand-alone airport servers with near-real-time replication to COC/CCC.

- Open and standards-based. IATA/ICAO messaging, web-service interfaces and the FAIRWAY bus keep the estate open and free of lock-in; EAC owns the data and admin rights.

- Secure and sovereign. 100% on-premises on EAC-owned hardware, defence-in-depth network design and centrally-managed EDR.

## 4.  The RESA Integrated Airport Suite

The functional systems are not independent silos — they are members of one integrated RESA suite that shares a common data model and reference data. INFOPAX (AODB) is the heart of the estate: it owns the flight schedule and reference data and feeds RMS, VISTA (FIDS), BAGERA (BRS) and ON BOARD (LDCS). RMS allocation changes flow back to INFOPAX in real time and out to FIDS; CREWS (CUPPS) consumes RMS allocations to drive counter and gate rights; BAGERA reconciles bags against AODB flights and DCS baggage messages. Because these are products of the same vendor, the interfaces between them are native and pre-built rather than custom integrations, which is the single biggest driver of delivery speed and reliability in this proposal.

## 4.3  FAIRWAY Integration Bus

All inter-system and external communication is brokered by RESA FAIRWAY, a communication hub / enterprise service bus operating a hub-and-spoke model. FAIRWAY collects and publishes data through configurable connectors (AFTN, IATA Type-B/Type-X, web services, file, database and custom protocols), decoupling producers from consumers so that systems can be added, replaced or scaled without re-engineering the others. FAIRWAY provides message routing, transformation, prioritisation (e.g. MVT ahead of routine traffic), queuing with a quarantine path for unparseable messages, and full exchange security. The same bus carries the APOC data-exchange feeds (FAIRWAY DX) to WAISL's intelligence layer, and the near-real-time AODB replication to COC/CCC.

| Interface | Carried over FAIRWAY |
| --- | --- |
| AODB ⇄ FIDS / RMS / BRS / LDCS | Native real-time flight, resource and status synchronisation between RESA products. |
| CUPPS ⇄ RMS / AODB / BHS | Counter & gate rights, allocation, BSM to baggage handling. |
| External messaging | AFTN, IATA Type-B/Type-X, AIDX/FIXM for A-CDM, Timatic, slot/SSIM. |
| Billing / ERP / PA / VDGS / master clock | Operational and billing feeds to EAC enterprise and field systems. |
| APOC (FAIRWAY DX) | Event streams to the WAISL AIOP intelligence layer and dashboards. |
| COC/CCC replication | Near-real-time AODB replication for centralised supervision. |

## 4.4  Standardised Multi-Airport Deployment

A single reference architecture is deployed at all eight airports. Each site runs the same virtualised application stack on a clustered compute/storage platform spread across two physically-independent core rooms, with the same backup and DR design and the same network segmentation. This standardisation means a configuration template, patch or report built once is rolled out everywhere; spare-parts holdings are common; and staff are interchangeable across sites. The COC/CCC adds a central monitoring and operations tier that aggregates flight data, system metrics and operational KPIs from every airport while each airport retains independent operation.

## 4.5  High Availability and Disaster Recovery

Every system is delivered on a fully-redundant, clustered server architecture with automatic failover and no single point of failure, targeting the RFP's availability mandates (e.g. ≥99.9% for CUPPS, ≥99.5% for baggage tracking, 24×7 for AODB). Failover is automatic in both directions — when the primary server is restored, control returns without manual intervention and without loss of information, scenarios or capability. Each airport has centralised backup with configurable frequency and rapid restore; databases are protected and replicated; and degraded/stand-alone modes keep field operations (FIDS displays, BRS scanning, check-in) running during connectivity loss.

## 4.6  Data, Reporting and Analytics

Operational reporting is delivered by RESA's native report engines plus the CLEVER business-intelligence layer, separated from production databases and capable of feeding Power BI and Excel. This serves the extensive AODB/FIDS/BRS report catalogues (flight, delay, passenger, nationality, resource-utilisation and slot-audit reporting) and the APOC KPI catalogue. The APOC platform additionally maintains a medallion-style data lakehouse for the predictive and what-if workloads described in Section 12.

4.7  Common RESA Platform Features

All RESA products in this proposal share a common application framework and a single, consistent user interface. This common foundation simplifies training, administration and maintenance across the suite, and ensures a uniform operator experience whichever system is in use:

•  User-rights management: creation of user groups and individual accounts; per-screen create / modify / delete rights; selective enabling of specific features and buttons; ability to fully restrict screens, even in read-only mode.

•  Configurable layout: tabular data display with a dedicated input area; selectable ribbon or hamburger menus; tree-structure grouping; user-customisable columns; multi-screen navigation; and a choice of presentation styles, colours and logos.

•  Data input: multi-record editing, duplication, batch modification of a field across many records, and mandatory-field (Tab-key) navigation for fast mass updates.

•  Sorting and searching: AND/OR filters, multi-column sorting, instant prompt search, predefined and stored custom filters, and in-column value filters.

•  Export and printing: export of the on-screen view (sorted/filtered) to HTML, XLS, XML and TXT, clipboard export, column selection and print preview.

# 5.  CUPPS — Common Use Passenger Processing  ·  RESA CREWS

## 5.1  Scope and Understanding

The CUPPS scope is to supply, install, integrate, commission, operate and maintain a complete Common Use Passenger Processing System across the eight airports on a stand-alone topology (servers at each airport), enabling multiple airlines and handlers to share check-in and gate positions consecutively or simultaneously. The system must be certified against the latest CUPPS standard, remain CUTE-backward-compatible, support every common peripheral, integrate with RMS/AODB/FIDS/BRS/BHS, and deliver ≥99.9% availability with no single point of failure. CUSS self-service must be supported, including operation of 16 CUSS kiosks at Sharm El-Sheikh and Hurghada.

## 5.2  Solution — RESA CREWS and Standards Compliance

WAISL delivers CUPPS using RESA CREWS, a COTS common-use platform certified against the IATA RP 1797 (CUPPS 1.03) standard and backward-compatible with legacy CUTE applications, hardware and protocols on the same architecture. CREWS is built on a server/client architecture, certified on Windows 11 64-bit, and complies with the full set of cited IATA recommended practices:

- RP 1797 / 1745 — CUPPS and baggage sorting/reconciliation messaging.

- RP 740 / 769 — baggage tag description; RP 722c/d/e — boarding-pass format and paper; RP 792 — bar-coded boarding passes (BCBP, PDF417 2D).

- Alignment with A4A TP 30.201 and ACI/ACTI RP 500A07, and readiness for IATA StB, One ID, NFC, RF baggage tagging and future biometrics.

CREWS arrives with a large library of pre-integrated, certified airline DCS packages; new airline integration is carried out jointly with the airline and the CREWS team per IATA practice. Because no airline-specific software resides permanently on a workstation — DCS emulation is streamed from the server for the duration of a flight and removed afterwards — workstations are fully common and interchangeable, so a faulty unit can be swapped by a local technician without reconfiguration.

## 5.3  Functional Description

CREWS provides the modules required by the RFP and exposes them through an intuitive GUI with full administration, supervision and maintenance tooling:

- CREWS Administration — central configuration and system administration, reachable from any workstation subject to administrator authentication; user and group management with authorisation levels, encrypted passwords, automatic logon timeout and single sign-on to workstations and host applications.

- CREWS Peripheral Manager — the device-interface layer presenting the live status of every peripheral (BTP, ATB/BPP, BGR, OCR/MSR, DCP), enabling first-line troubleshooting, remote test and the ability to send to a peripheral connected on another workstation.

- Video/DCS emulation — multiple airline applications run concurrently on a workstation over TCP/IP or legacy protocols (MATIP supported via gateway); back-office positions run CREWS as a standard Windows application alongside office tools.

- CREWS Reporting / Statistics — connection time, boarding-pass and bag-tag counts, document-printer characters, gate re-reads, time-of-use per user, plus operational KPIs (average check-in time per airline/counter, gate-reader failures) for CCO reporting and airport invoicing.

- Real-time operational dashboard for the CCO showing the status of all workstations, peripherals and active airline sessions, with colour-coded alerts and push notifications on critical failures.

- BSM generation to the BHS, FIDS client for display management of desks/gates from CUPPS workstations, and RFID-ready bag-tag printing for the future.

## 5.4  Peripherals

All peripherals are supplied to (or above) the RFP minimum specifications, driven natively by the CREWS peripheral manager:

| Peripheral | Proposed device | Compliance highlights |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Workstation | Enterprise 24×7 PC, Core i7 (latest gen), 16 GB RAM, ≥256 GB SSD, GbE, 24" LED | Designed for continuous airport operation; fully common/interchangeable. |
| Baggage Tag Printer (BTP) | Custom TK180 Metal (AEA, hot-swap) | Direct thermal ≥203 dpi, RFID per IATA 753, ≥3 ips, generic & airline stock. |
| Boarding Pass / ATB Printer | TK180 Metal (combined BPP/BTP) | Two-way host comms, CUTE & generic mode, downloadable logos, ≤5 s/encode. |
| Boarding Gate Reader (BGR) | DESKO BGR 504 pro | BCBP & mobile read <1 s, AEA PECTAB, red/green optical indicators, NFC-ready. |
| Passport / OCR Reader | DESKO NEPTUN chrom (OCR+MSR) | All OCR passport types, ISO charset, MTBF ≥100,000 h, first-read ≥95%, encryptable. |
| Document Printer (DCP) | Impact dot-matrix, 3–10" paper | ≥203 dpi, ≥3 ips, dual feed, long ribbon/print-head life. |
| CUSS Self-Service | RESA SKYCHECK kiosk (16 units SSH/HRG) | Certified CUSS; replaceable at no cost if inoperable per Section 5. |

## 5.5  Architecture, Availability and Integration

The CREWS central platform is a multiple-clustered, server-based system with no single point of failure: an automatic failover server takes control on primary failure and returns control automatically on restore, with no loss of information or scenarios and full transparency to users — delivering the mandated ≥99.9% availability for 24×7 operation. Workstations are mutually independent and support peripherals over USB/serial. CREWS integrates over the FAIRWAY bus with RMS (automatic assignment/removal of counter and gate rights from real-time schedules and airline contracts, with CCO override), AODB, FIDS, BRS and the BHS, and is ready to integrate self-boarding gates, self bag-drop and biometric components as future enhancements. The platform supports smooth, tested release deployment with a separate test environment to protect day-to-day operations.

# 6.  LDCS — Local Departure Control System  ·  RESA ON BOARD

## 6.1  Scope and Understanding

The LDCS scope is to design, supply, integrate, commission, operate and maintain a Local Departure Control System on a stand-alone topology at all eight airports, providing complete passenger check-in, boarding and weight-and-balance for airlines and ground handlers. It must support fast bag-drop, self-boarding and advanced boarding modules; integrate real-time Timatic document checking and airline reservation systems (e.g. Navitaire New Skies); guarantee operation with the CUPPS platform; and deliver class-leading, easy-to-learn passenger handling with fast processing times.

## 6.2  Solution — RESA ON BOARD and Evionica Weight & Balance

WAISL delivers LDCS using RESA ON BOARD, a full departure-control system covering flight preparation, cabin-plan creation, passenger management, check-in and boarding, with post-processing and monitoring. ON BOARD is a standardised system that runs natively on the CREWS CUPPS platform, satisfying the guarantee to operate with CUPPS. Weight-and-balance and load control are provided by the integrated Evionica Weight & Balance SaaS, a pilot-and-engineer-designed platform delivering automated, compliant load sheets with >99% availability. The user interface and date/time formats are in English, with automatic screen-saver and logoff security timeouts.

## 6.3  Functional Description

- Passenger processing — single and group check-in, manual/automatic seat selection and reseating, graphical seat-map with sub-classes, upgrade/downgrade, standby and wait-listing, frequent-flyer update, SSR and sub-SSR handling, go-show/rec passengers, and full 2D-barcode boarding-pass and bag-tag printing.

- Documentation to authorities — passport reading and APIS/APP reporting to governmental authorities, with API country support and new countries added within two weeks; provider-managed Type-B and mail connectivity.

- Timatic integration — real-time Timatic and Timatic AutoCheck access from within ON BOARD, giving check-in agents accurate travel-document advice, reducing fines and queue times, with a simple traffic-light indication and detailed responses.

- Reservation integration — bidirectional integration with airline reservation systems (e.g. Navitaire New Skies) so data flows automatically between DCS and reservation without Type-B, with passenger details transferred at a configurable time.

- Load control (W&B) — automated weight-and-balance producing loading message (LDM), loading instruction (LIR), load sheet and container-distribution message (CPM) per the airline's aircraft data, for loaders and flight crew.

- Operations — unlimited concurrent airlines, 2- and 3-letter airline codes, all baggage categories (crew, rush, etc.), free PECTAB and GUI editor/designer, and EAC-customised monthly PAX reporting.

# 7.  AODB — Airport Operational Database  ·  RESA INFOPAX

## 7.1  Scope and Understanding

The AODB is the operational heart of each airport and the most extensively specified system in the RFP (337 requirements). The scope is to supply, integrate, commission, operate and maintain an Airport Operational Database / Flight Information Management System (AODB/FIMS) on a stand-alone topology at all eight airports, with near-real-time central replication to COC/CCC so supervisors can view any airport's AODB without switching systems and coordinate cross-airport events such as diversions. The AODB must natively interface FIDS, RMS, LDCS and BRS, process AFTN and IATA Type-B/Type-X messaging, manage seasonal and operational flight schedules, support A-CDM, and deliver a comprehensive reporting and statistics catalogue. EAC requires the AODB pre-populated with five years of airline data and the database standardised across all airports before go-live.

## 7.2  Solution — RESA INFOPAX (FIMS)

WAISL delivers the AODB using RESA INFOPAX, a mature flight-information-management system that provides a single, authoritative source of flight truth feeding every other system in the estate. INFOPAX models airport data using recognisable IATA/ICAO structures, supports an unlimited number of clients and connections, runs reliably 24×7, and lets administrators extend attributes and data schemes without vendor development. Data follows IATA/ICAO standards throughout, with switching between IATA and ICAO codes in every field, UTC and local time, English date/time formats and automatic daylight-saving handling.

## 7.3  Functional Description

- Reference data — full airport, airline, handler, aircraft-type, registration, country, terminal, counter, gate, stand, belt, zone and stopover reference data, with extra configurable fields, providing the comprehensible airport data model the RFP requires.

- Seasonal & operational scheduling — import of seasonal schedules in IATA SSIM (latest version, backward-compatible) or SCORE format with verify-and-correct before storage; automatic creation of the operational schedule from the seasonal for a configurable horizon; manual and automatic updates through an interactive graphical interface with conflict checking, flight joining/splitting, rotations and validity periods; table and Gantt views with user-defined filters and saved patterns.

- Message processing — automatic ingestion of AFTN (FPL, ARR, DEP) and IATA Type-B messages, processing MVT (AHM 780), LDM (AHM 516), SLS (AHM 588), PTM/PSM (AHM 503), PAL/CAL (PSCRM), with message prioritisation rules (MVT ahead of routine), a queue viewer for the CCO and a quarantine path for unparseable messages rather than silent approval.

- Real-time flight management — a colour-coded operational dashboard and watch-list tracking flight states (landed, on-block, off-block, etc.); resource follow-up for belts, counters, gates, stands, exits and access means; ETA/ETD monitoring; diversion, aircraft-change, delay and cancellation handling with operational alerts during irregular operations.

- Flow forecasting — predicted passenger numbers for departures (at security) and arrivals, and predicted departure bags, configurable by fill rate and flow typology, improvable with machine learning on real data, graphically visualised with Excel export and manual CCO override.

- A-CDM — configurable milestone process, TOBT and TSAT management, with finished flights archived yet accessible for statistics and invoicing.

- Archive & audit — complete flight and log archive with fast search on historical flights, full change tracking (user, device, time, logs) filterable by flight or user, and a manual archive override to keep a flight under investigation visible.

- Native dark mode & modern GUI — a built-in (non-plugin) dark mode and a modern, tile-based, dashboard-driven interface with colour-coded statuses (e.g. orange for delayed, distinct colour for cancelled) and Gantt display of RMS allocations, plus mobile/tablet interfaces for apron and area controllers.

### 7.3.1  Reporting and Statistics

INFOPAX, with the CLEVER BI layer, delivers the full report catalogue required by the RFP — including flights by airline, movements by hour, passengers by airline/hour/nationality, delay summaries and delay-code analysis, per-flight PAX and load factor, resource usage and stand/gate/counter/belt utilisation, and slot audit — with real-time dashboards plus daily, weekly and monthly outputs, SQL-based ad-hoc queries, and export to Excel, HTML, flat file and PDF.

## 7.4  Resilience, Replication and Integration

Each airport's INFOPAX runs on redundant servers with automatic application and database backup (configurable frequency) and server-failover recovery. Local airport operation continues if the COC/CCC link fails; on restoration, near-real-time replication re-synchronises the central view. Data exchange with RMS and FIDS is real-time and automatic — a resource allocation made in RMS is reflected immediately across AODB and FIDS without manual intervention. INFOPAX also integrates the first-bag/last-bag capability for SSH, HRG and HBE, billing/ERP, APP/APIS, master clock, PA, VDGS, inspection and lost-and-found systems, and includes FlightAware data.

| 116 | — | AODB/FIMS must have functionality of creating and updating of seasonal, periodical and operative flight schedules. FIMS provides possibility of manual and automatic creation of flight schedule. Manual creation means that operator enters data on received flight announcements into a tabular form and automatic creation means integration with Telex systems for AFTN or Type B. Other information from these telexes such as FPL, ARR, DEP messages of AFTN or MVT, LDM from type B are to be processed and distribute to other systems. This distribution shall trigger automatically the change/update of information on FIDS. AODB/FIMS must enable user to define automatic update mode (for example mode of automatic update and notification of user on completed update or mode of notification of user on received modification - user make changes manually and similar) | Comply | Comply via RESA INFOPAX. INFOPAX processes AFTN/Type-B (MVT, LDM, SLS, PTM, PAL/CAL) and imports IATA SSIM seasonal schedules. |

# 8.  RMS — Resource Management System  ·  RESA INFOPAX-RMS

## 8.1  Scope and Understanding

The RMS scope is to supply, integrate, commission, operate and maintain a Resource Management System on a stand-alone topology at all eight airports, with mirrored real-time supervision at COC/CCC that can assume control only if local controllers are unreachable. The RMS plans and allocates airport resources — parking stands, gates, check-in and transfer counters, baggage belts and passport control — in two phases (resource planning and real-time management), integrates tightly with AODB, FIDS and BRS, and must scale from an initial 12 million to 25 million passengers per annum without full reconfiguration or downtime beyond four approved hours.

## 8.2  Solution — RESA INFOPAX-RMS

WAISL delivers RMS using RESA INFOPAX-RMS, a fully-integrated module of the INFOPAX family that automatically inherits the resources configured in the AODB, so allocation works on a single shared data model. INFOPAX-RMS is an intuitive, user-friendly tool offering manual and automatic allocation, resource-dependency and consistency checks, standard-week templates, occupancy statistics, what-if and what-else functions, and a degraded mode — exactly the capabilities the RFP enumerates. All times are shown in local airport time with UTC in parentheses, with automatic daylight-saving handling that never loses ongoing allocations.

## 8.3  Functional Description

- Gantt allocation — critical resources (stands/tubs, gates, check-in, belts, passport control) are allocated on a drag-and-drop Gantt with buffer times, a live current-time marker, zoom, vertical resource / horizontal time navigation, and per-user colour-coding rules; reallocation to a new resource is a maximum two-step action with automatic conflict detection.

- Conflict & warning management — flights with no allocation or conflicted allocation are highlighted in distinct warning colours; alerts cover over-sizing, unavailable resources and arrival/departure registration mismatches; automatic time-scrolling keeps the workspace on the current time.

- Real-time AODB linkage — RMS automatically allocates scheduled resources to new real-time flights; every modification updates the AODB in real time and propagates to FIDS; a daily reconciliation report flags resources present in AODB but not RMS for the CCO to import or ignore.

- Supervisor control — a built-in Supervisor Override role can pause automatic allocation with a single click during irregular operations; all manual overrides require a mandatory reason code (medical emergency, apron obstruction, VIP, etc.), are logged, and are visible to other controllers in real time.

- Contingency & IROPS — what-if scenario simulation, temporary resource closure, contingency allocation planning, and delay/diversion/cancellation/aircraft-change handling.

- Audit & redundancy — full audit trail of every allocation change for each flight from −72 h to +24 h, user-activity tracking, database backup and server failover ensuring continuity; automatic-logoff is disabled while a live Gantt or unresolved alerts are displayed.

# 9.  FIDS — Flight Information Display System  ·  RESA VISTA

## 9.1  Scope and Understanding

The FIDS scope is to supply, integrate, commission, operate and maintain a Flight Information Display System on a stand-alone topology at all eight airports with centralised supervision at COC/CCC. FIDS presents flight and operational information to passengers and staff, must operate stand-alone if upstream systems are down, must support central emergency override of any display or group of displays (including advertisements), and must provide unified templates across airports. Per Section 5, WAISL is responsible for all airport display screens branded under FIDS within the concession.

## 9.2  Solution — RESA VISTA

WAISL delivers FIDS using RESA VISTA, a display-management platform covering screen management, multilingual content, rule-driven data presentation, image creation, broadcast and supervision. VISTA applies configurable rules to AODB data before display (e.g. updating information, delaying a gate display, automatic deletions on AODB events), supports unlimited scalability simply by adding display devices, and is administered remotely over the network with real-time device monitoring. The interface and date/time formats are English, with screen-saver and automatic-logoff security timeouts and secured workstations.

## 9.3  Functional Description

- Multilingual display — at least five languages including Arabic and English, switchable on one screen or shown simultaneously on different screens, with EAC able to minimise the language set via the administration tool.

- Content design — a content-design application integral to VISTA supporting text formatting, graphics/audio/video and web streaming, multi-media and scheduled content, screen-saver scheduling, page-in-page composition, transparent and background images, airline-logo management, dynamic image creation, weather/meteorological data, and complex images combining flight and non-flight content (flight list + bus list + weather) — with on-screen preview before sending and retrieval of archived images.

- Templates & layouts — predefined patterns for check-in, gate, arrivals, departures and combinations, plus user-designed patterns; video walls; synchronised display change within a screen group; conditional element display; default images when no information is available; unified templates across all airports per Section 5.

- Gate & counter control — a web application (operable from CREWS CUPPS workstations after agent authentication, including a password-only path for non-computerised airlines) lets agents set gate status (Open / Boarding / Final Call / Close), counter status (Open / Close), free or predefined text and video streams; reclaim-belt displays let staff set first-bag/last-bag status.

- Emergency & override — a CCO emergency-broadcast button forces full-screen multilingual emergency messages on any display or group, overriding all content including advertising, with integration to evacuation systems; the CCO can remotely restart any display or group.

- Health monitoring & continuity — a real-time CCO dashboard shows each display's health (online/offline/last-update) and any active override; screen-offline, media-player-failure and network alerts; automatic restart of failed devices; and a local database that sustains display and synchronises latest flight data during AODB-connectivity loss, with manual override and full manual entry as a fallback. A standard HTML5 web interface and LED-service templates are provided.

# 10.  BRS — Baggage Reconciliation System  ·  RESA BAGERA

## 10.1  Scope and Understanding

The BRS scope is to supply, integrate, commission, operate and maintain a Baggage Reconciliation System on a stand-alone topology at all eight airports with centralised supervision at COC/CCC. The BRS must comply with IATA RP 1745/1800, ICAO Annex 17, ECAC Document 30 and IATA Resolution 753; provide real-time, decision-support baggage reconciliation on wireless rugged handheld terminals; store detailed position and status for every bag; operate in a multi-airport, multi-handler environment; offer a degraded/offline mode; and integrate with AODB, RMS, DCS/CUPPS, sorters and a message-distribution system.

## 10.2  Solution — RESA BAGERA and BAGWAY

WAISL delivers BRS using RESA BAGERA, a real-time baggage-reconciliation and tracking solution covering flight preparation, the baggage-loading area, tracking, runway and arrivals, with a supervision suite, handheld scanners, administration and analytics. BAGERA is IATA Resolution 753 compliant — providing the four mandated tracking points and ramp/arrival coverage — and lets handlers decide in real time whether a bag may be loaded based on IATA baggage messages and AODB flight information. Where EAC needs an airport message-distribution system, RESA BAGWAY provides the MDS, delivering all required BSMs from airlines to the airport; BAGERA can equally interface any third-party MDS chosen by the airport.

## 10.3  Functional Description

- Loading & reconciliation — container allocation to flights (sorted by class/destination), container-tag printing to IATA A4 standard, container opening by scan, bag loading via HHT with light/sound accept-reject feedback and reject-cause display, mono/multi-class and shared check-in, crew bags, transfers between containers, container merges, rush-bag creation with new-tag printing, and bag offload confirmed by scanning the tag.

- Tracking & status — by tag number, passenger name or reference the operator sees a bag's full status and characteristics (flight, passenger, checked-in/boarded), container status and rank in the hold, and processing-flight information; arrival handling and bulk-baggage loading with hold-location capture are supported.

- Handheld terminals — rugged Android HHTs (shock/dust/water resistant, multiple 1.5 m drops), Wi-Fi and 4G/5G SIM for ramp and arrival areas without Wi-Fi, optional RFID, with full BRS functionality on the HHT (create flights, search, print, daily tracking) and an offline mode that buffers work and alerts on reconnection. Scanners are remotely upgraded, located and supervised from the administration workstation, and allocated to handlers by visual (headshot) identification.

- Messaging & interfaces — receives and handles BSM, BPM, BUM, BIM, BCM and sends BNS/BPM; manages every flight-change scenario (BSM before/after flight creation, VIA destinations, wrong-destination correction, destination change, delete/restore); integrates AODB for real-time flight data, DCS/CUPPS for connecting-bag BSMs, sorters (BPM per sorted bag), World Tracer (FWD) and email despatch of manifests/reports.

- Supervision & reporting — single-screen flight, passenger, container and HHT views; historical baggage information (rejections, missing, damaged, rush, manifests); reports for bags per flight/airline/operator, first/last bag in ULD, processing time, show time, bags per agent, average loading duration and handling errors; data online for at least seven days then archived for months; administrator-defined statistics auto-emailed; a dedicated VIEWER workstation for arrival/departure baggage details.

# 11. APOC - Airport Operations Command Centre

## Architecture

| Layer | What it does |
| --- | --- |
| Ingestion | Captures every event from EAC’s systems (AODB/INFOPAX, RMS, FIDS, BRS, CUPPS, LDCS, A-CDM, slots, BHS) and external feeds through FAIRWAY DX connectors. |
| Streaming & correlation | Correlates flight, turnaround, resource, passenger-flow and baggage events into a single, entity-resolved operational object in real time. |
| Data platform (lakehouse) | Stores the live operational observation history in a medallion lakehouse (Bronze/Silver/Gold) for analytics, ML and what-if. |
| Intelligence | Forecasting, A-CDM milestone prediction, anomaly detection and what-if simulation; models retrain automatically on real data. |
| Presentation (MFE) | Role-specific micro-front-end dashboards and portals for the AOCC, airlines, ground handlers, baggage and terminal teams. |

### Medallion Lakehouse

- AiOP implements  three-tier Lakehouse architecture-

Bronze  immutable raw store Every message and event is captured exactly as received, providing an auditable,  record of operational truth.

normalised, entity-resolved Raw events are cleaned, standardised to IATA/ICAO models and resolved to canonical flight, resource and passenger entities, producing one consistent operational object.

KPI and analytics-ready Curated, aggregated datasets power the dashboards, the KPI catalogue, the forecasting models and exported reports.

# 12.  Baggage Tracking & Lost-and-Found  ·  RESA PAXTRACK

## 12.1  Scope and Understanding

The Baggage Tracking scope is a centralised platform that lets airport operations, airlines, ground handlers and customer-service teams manage lost, delayed, misrouted, unidentified, left-behind and found baggage while improving passenger experience, operational efficiency, security and traceability. It must provide real-time baggage tracking for passengers, secure baggage-storage monitoring, chain-of-custody management, reporting dashboards, SLA monitoring and integration with airport operational systems, delivered as a complete solution (software, implementation, integration, training, support) including 22 rugged Android devices and ≥99.5% availability.

## 12.2  Solution — RESA PAXTRACK

WAISL delivers baggage tracking using RESA PAXTRACK, a passenger-and-baggage tracking platform with administration, supervision, access control and a biometric-ready journey. PAXTRACK is web-based and mobile-compatible, supports cloud and on-premises deployment (delivered on-premises here), is multilingual (minimum English and Arabic), provides a scalable architecture with real-time monitoring, and complements BAGERA's reconciliation by owning the passenger-facing and lost-&-found lifecycle.

## 12.3  Functional Description

- Lost-&-found and property management — registration of lost, delayed, misrouted and unidentified baggage; baggage lifecycle and real-time status; storage-location management with baggage-aging alerts; chain-of-custody and audit trail.

- Passenger services — real-time baggage tracking for passengers, notifications by email or mobile app, claim management, and search by tag, flight, passenger name or reference number.

- Devices & data capture — integration with handheld scanners and the 22 supplied rugged Android tablets (Android 13+, ≥8 GB RAM, ≥128 GB storage, ≥10" display, dust/water resistant, camera), with 1D and 2D barcode support.

- Security & operations — secure role-based access, audit logging, backup mechanism, an operations-monitoring dashboard, reporting and analytics, and SLA monitoring with alerts; ≥99.5% availability.

- Integration — AODB, airline/DCS systems, mobile apps and web portals, and reporting/BI platforms, over the FAIRWAY bus.

# 13.  Infrastructure & Cybersecurity

## 13.1  Standardised Airport Infrastructure

A single, standardised infrastructure architecture is deployed across all eight airports to support the mission-critical operational systems with high availability, resilience, performance and future scalability. Each airport provides two physically-independent Core Rooms (Core 1 & Core 2) in a high-availability arrangement interconnected by a fibre backbone, eliminating any single point of failure. Compute and storage use a fully-redundant clustered platform with workload mobility and automatic failover across both core rooms; each datacentre deploys two datacentre-grade top-of-rack switches for redundant high-speed connectivity and secure segmentation of management, storage and virtual networks. Local redundancy (dual power, UPS, redundant NICs, RAID) is complemented by centralised backup with replication, retention and rapid recovery.

13.1.1  RESA Server Infrastructure (Per Airport)

Each airport runs a standardised, high-availability RESA server platform deployed across two independent core rooms. Two physical servers per site run Windows Server 2025 Standard with the Hyper-V Type-1 hypervisor and Windows Server Failover Clustering (WSFC), Microsoft SQL Server 2025, and the DataCore hyper-converged (virtualised-SAN) solution for storage high availability. Together these provide both physical and software redundancy: any virtual machine can run on either physical server, ensuring continuity through hardware failure, planned maintenance and service failover.

The platform follows a duplicated three-tier architecture, with every role hosted on dedicated, duplicated virtual machines: Domain Controllers (×2 — Active Directory authentication, GPO, DNS, DHCP, redundant); SQL database servers (×2 clustered nodes — failover limited to instance restart on the second node with no data loss); application servers (×2 clustered, using Cluster Shared Volumes / shared disks); and web/IIS application servers (×2 clustered). Resiliency is reinforced through Cluster Shared Volumes, shared storage, quorum mechanisms, virtual IP addressing and role-based service separation. Standard and large sizing models (CPU, RAM, disk, IOPS, throughput) are defined per airport according to traffic, number of RESA products, data volume and security segmentation.

| Key point | Implemented through | Operational benefit |
| --- | --- | --- |
| High availability | Redundant physical servers, Hyper-V, WSFC, clustered SQL Server, clustered application and web services | Continuity of airport operations during hardware failure, planned maintenance or service failover |
| Resiliency | Cluster Shared Volumes, shared storage, quorum, virtual IP addressing, duplicated VMs and role separation | Reduced service interruption, controlled failover and improved data integrity |
| Performance | Defined CPU, RAM, disk, IOPS and throughput sizing for standard and large configurations | Predictable capacity and stable response times for databases, applications and users |
| Future scalability | Adaptable VM sizing, extensible storage, architecture aligned to traffic and product scope | Supports growth, additional RESA products, higher data volumes and multi-site scenarios |
| Standardised architecture | Windows Server, Hyper-V, WSFC, SQL Server, dedicated VM roles, monitoring, antivirus and backup | Simplified, repeatable deployment and consistent operations across all eight airports |
| Monitoring & backup | Third-party monitoring compatibility, RESA supervision modules, centralised antivirus, SQL maintenance plans, disk-and-tape backup | Improved visibility, faster troubleshooting and controlled recovery |

Monitoring: because the platform uses widely-adopted Microsoft technologies, all elements can be monitored by third-party tools (e.g. Zabbix); in addition, each RESA application provides a built-in supervision module for remote oversight of CUPPS workstations, peripherals, BRS scanners and FIDS terminals, with license/version tracking and log retrieval.

Backup: a dedicated third physical server hosts backup management (and the ESET antivirus VM). RESA proposes Arcserve UDP in a Disk-to-Disk-to-Tape (D2D2T) strategy — hot data backed up to disk for fast recovery and cold data to tape for secure offline retention — with daily backup jobs and SQL maintenance plans.

## 13.3  Endpoint Protection & Cybersecurity

A centrally-managed, enterprise-grade Endpoint Protection / EDR platform covers all servers and client workstations in scope, based on CrowdStrike (or an EAC-approved equivalent enterprise-class EDR/XDR), providing real-time threat prevention and detection, behavioural analysis and advanced threat analytics, automated signature/engine/policy updates, centralised monitoring, reporting and incident management, and threat response and containment, across Windows workstation and server operating systems. The wider security architecture follows defence-in-depth — network segmentation and firewalling, platform hardening, application security, data protection and PII handling, identity and access management, and audit/logging — mapped to EAC ICT policy, ECAA and NCSA NIS requirements.

## 13.4  Centralised COC/CCC Monitoring

A centralised monitoring and control platform at EAC COC/CCC aggregates operational data, flight information and system metrics from each airport, giving network-wide visibility while each airport retains operational independence. This platform underpins the central supervision of AODB, RMS, FIDS and BRS and the consolidated KPI reporting, and hosts the operations centre with the LED video walls specified in Appendix 4.

Each airport (SSH, HRG, HBE, LXR, ASW, HMB, ATZ and ABS) hosts the same standardised local high-availability RESA infrastructure and remains fully autonomous for its mission-critical systems even if VPN/CCC connectivity is temporarily lost. The central CCC connects to every airport through dedicated VPN tunnels and provides a consolidated supervision layer that can:

•  Monitor local service availability (SQL instances, application services, IIS web services, domain controllers, supervision modules and critical RESA components).

•  Check infrastructure health — VM status, cluster-node availability, failover state, storage and CSV usage, CPU/memory/disk, IOPS and network connectivity.

•  Supervise VPN connectivity to each airport (tunnel availability, latency, loss of visibility) and centralise alerts, events, logs and traces for faster incident qualification.

•  Verify backup execution, SQL maintenance plans, retention and restore points; follow antivirus/security status; and track software versions, licenses and configuration consistency across sites.

•  Support preventive maintenance (planned checks, patch-window coordination, service-restart validation) and provide consolidated reporting and dashboards across all sites.

# 14.  Training

RESA and WAISL deliver a complete training programme in English for all systems. Operations training is conducted on-site (EAC provides the training room; WAISL provides everything else), is heavily hands-on, and covers all operational procedures, normal mode, common commands and features, every degraded mode, and start-up/shutdown procedures, so that EAC staff can operate each system independently without contractor support. A full set of training documents is provided to each trainee in hard copy and on memory stick by qualified, experienced trainers.

| Training element | Provision |
| --- | --- |
| Operator training (EAC staff) | 10 trainees per airport — 80 trainees total — across all systems |
| Airline staff training | 5 trainees per airport, including LDCS and Weight & Balance users |
| Overseas technical workshop | 10 EAC trainees; site visits on special airport systems worldwide; WAISL funds accommodation, flights, local transport and visas |

# 15.  General & Commercial Compliance (Sections 4–10)

WAISL, as bidder, with RESA as the authorised system owner, complies with the general, eligibility, commitment, technical, payment, penalty, general-terms and warranty conditions of the RFP. Highlights:

- Eligibility (Sec. 4) — specialist airport-systems experience across AODB/FIDS/RMS/CUPPS/LDCS/BRS; reference projects exceeding 30 m passengers and 400+ counters; biometric/Bluetooth upgrade path; RESA letter of authorisation and five-year support commitment.

- Commitments (Sec. 5) — full mandatory site survey of all airports; supply and operation of 16 CUSS kiosks at SSH/HRG; AODB pre-populated with five years of airline data and standardised across airports; CUPPS interfaced with BHS and integrated with LDCS at Alexandria; unified FIDS templates; responsibility for all FIDS-branded screens; operations centre with LED video walls per Appendix 4; zero downtime during execution; full migration plan.

- SLA staffing (Sec. 5) — the on-site engineer/technician matrix at every airport and 3 engineers at COC/CCC (Section 3.1), with Level-1 and Level-2 staff qualified and certified as required (Bachelor in Engineering/CS; CCNA certified, CCNP trained for Level-2).

- Technical & commercial (Sec. 6–8) — technical and financial offers structured as required, MS Project schedule covering Delivery, Installation, Integration/Functional Tests, OAT, SPA, GPA and Training; acceptance of the payment milestones (20% advance / 40% shipping / 40% on initial acceptance), the device and system-wide downtime penalty schedule, and the up-to-10% monthly SLA deduction.

- General terms & warranty (Sec. 9–10) — fully-redundant HA architecture with no single point of failure; a detailed compliance matrix (this document); and a 2-year warranty on all hardware and software after GPA, with a five-year SLA from initial acceptance.

# 16.  Implementation, Migration & Service

## 16.1  Delivery Approach and Milestones

Delivery is 4–6 months from notification/advance payment, with per-site installation completed within 2 months of obstacle-free site hand-over. The programme is managed to the RFP milestone set, evidenced by an MS Project schedule:

| Milestone | Description |
| --- | --- |
| Delivery | Manufacture, supply and shipment of hardware and software (foreign shipments CIP Cairo Airport in EAC's name). |
| Site installation | Per-airport installation within 2 months of obstacle-free hand-over. |
| Interfaces / Integration & functional tests | RESA-native and external interfaces configured over FAIRWAY and function-tested. |
| OAT | Operational Acceptance Test with tools and documentation provided before commissioning. |
| SPA | Site Provisional Acceptance — triggers SLA commencement for that site. |
| GPA | Global Provisional Acceptance — starts the 2-year warranty across the estate. |
| Training | Operator, airline and overseas-workshop training per Section 14. |

## 16.2  Migration — Zero Downtime

WAISL provides a full migration plan for replacing the existing systems without operational disruption. Standardised, pre-staged airport builds, parallel running and cut-over during low-traffic windows, combined with the stand-alone topology and RESA's native interfaces, allow each system to be commissioned and switched over with zero downtime, coordinated with airport management and the supervision team, under the certified hand-over-minute regime in Section 5.

## 16.3  Five-Year SLA

From initial acceptance, WAISL operates a five-year SLA with the on-site staffing matrix at every airport (Section 3.1), one spare unit per device type, a comprehensive monthly maintenance offer covering servers, devices and consumables, and spare-parts replacement within three months where the manufacturer cannot supply. The SLA accepts the RFP's device and system-wide downtime penalties and the up-to-10% monthly performance-based deduction, with RESA underwriting product support for the full term.

16.4  Technical Assistance

RESA's technical assistance, coordinated with WAISL, covers: project management and coordination; pre-configuration of servers in RESA's laboratory; environment configuration; any specific/additional developments; software installation, configuration, set-up and commissioning; administrator/trainer training and knowledge transfer; documentation; validation testing; and assistance during cut-over (including travel and related expenses). A detailed Work Breakdown, provisional schedule and RACI matrix are provided as appendices.

16.5  Software Maintenance & 24/7 Third-Level Support

RESA software licenses are perpetual and include software maintenance for the first year. The systems are designed to be self-operated and self-maintained by the airport IT team (or a contracted local team), with powerful administration and remote-management tools — so no permanent on-site RESA engineers are required once live. Third-level maintenance covers corrective and remote maintenance plus the delivery of all new versions, including major upgrades such as operating-system migrations, at no additional licensing cost. A 24/7 third-level technical support service is included specifically to meet the SLA required by the tender.

16.6  Commercial & Contractual Terms

Licensing: RESA licenses are perpetual (unlimited in time); once purchased by EAC they remain available indefinitely, with the first year of warranty/maintenance included in the licence cost and an annual maintenance fee thereafter (per the financial quotation).

Payment terms (proposed): Hardware — 30% down payment at order, 70% on shipment. Software and technical assistance — 30% down payment at order, 60% before installation, 10% after cut-over. Payment by L/C within 30 days of invoice date.

Training: delivered on a train-the-trainers basis for system administrators (recommended maximum five participants per session), at the first three airports with RESA engineers and at RESA's premises in France with the EAC team; each session combines formal presentation, demonstration and hands-on practice on real systems.

16.7  Scope Boundaries and Prerequisites

Out of RESA scope (indicative): Ethernet/Wi-Fi/3G-4G, RF and power-supply networks; furniture supply/installation; power and IT wiring; test equipment and special tools; disassembly/disposal/recycling of existing equipment; physical installation and deployment of workstations, peripherals and display terminals; non-quoted COTS software and equipment; Internet connectivity; duties and taxes; consumables beyond those quoted; peripheral-firmware USB upgrades; DCS routers, configuration and communication lines (airlines' responsibility); and any BSM Message Distribution System other than the included BAGWAY.

Customer prerequisites (indicative): a clean, dust-free environment with stable, redundant power and operational cooling; prepared core room(s) with secured access and validated power/network cabling ready for the RESA servers; an Internet connection and, preferably, VPN access for RESA remote installation, integration testing, maintenance and updates; hardware delivered on-site and cleared through customs; defined equipment locations; completed prerequisite documents; and airport (or third-party) IT and provider staff available for training, physical installation and interface/integration testing.

# 17.  Supply, Deployment, Operations & Local Support

This section consolidates the supply, site-preparation, deployment, operations and local-support scope that completes WAISL’s turnkey delivery to EAC across Sharm El-Sheikh and the network of eight airports. WAISL delivers this scope through its integrated delivery team under a single contract and remains the single point of accountability to EAC.

## 17.4  Operations & Support Model ( L3)

WAISL provides a tiered support model across the five-year term, combining in-country first- and second-line operations with specialist third-line software support, all governed centrally by WAISL against the RFP service levels and presented to EAC as a single WAISL-operated service.

RESA–WAISL delivers a three-tier support model across the five-year SLA term, combining in-country first- and second-line operations with specialist third-line product engineering, governed centrally against the RFP service levels and the on-site staffing matrix (Section 3.1). The tiers and their responsibilities are:

| Tier | Function | Responsible party |
| --- | --- | --- |
| Level 1 — On-site first line | First point of contact, initial triage, basic troubleshooting and ticket logging at each airport | RESA–WAISL on-site teams (per RFP staffing matrix) |
| Level 2 — Technical & escalation management | Incident ownership, escalation management and coordination between first line and product engineering; in-country technical bridge based in Cairo | RESA–WAISL in-country team |
| Level 3 — Product / OEM engineering | Vendor-specific deep technical fixes, code-level changes, configuration and hardware replacement | RESA product engineering |

#### In-Country Level 3 Support Management

A dedicated in-country team based in Cairo provides round-the-clock (24×7×365) Level 3 support management, acting as the technical bridge between the first/second-line operations and RESA product engineering. The team has hands-on airport-IT expertise across the systems in scope and established coordination channels with the system OEMs to accelerate escalations.

| System | Representative support activities |
| --- | --- |
| DCS / LDCS — Departure Control | Workstation/printer faults, interface errors, boarding-bridge integration issues |
| BHS — Baggage Handling | Controller alarms, sortation-logic and scanner/BRS interface faults |
| AODB — Operational Database | Data-sync errors, flight-record discrepancies, interface/API failures |
| FIDS — Flight Information Display | Display/content faults, feed interruptions, controller connectivity |
| CUPPS & CUSS — Common Use | Application faults, peripheral errors, session/user-profile issues |
| RMS — Resource Management | Allocation conflicts, scheduling-engine errors, interface issues |
| Network & Infrastructure | Connectivity faults, server/application performance, infrastructure alarms |

Core responsibilities:

Incident management — receiving, acknowledging and working all incidents escalated from first line within agreed SLAs.

Level 3 escalation — engaging OEM/product support for issues beyond second-line scope and tracking them through to closure.

Proactive monitoring — monitoring system health and logs to act on issues before they affect operations.

Knowledge management — maintaining runbooks, known-error records and resolution guides for recurring issues.

Change support — supporting planned maintenance, patch deployment and upgrade windows for in-scope systems.

Reporting & governance:

Daily — real-time visibility of open incidents, SLA status and escalations.

Weekly — ticket volumes, SLA performance, top recurring issues and trend analysis.

Monthly — SLA-compliance review, root-cause summaries and improvement actions, presented in a formal governance meeting.

#### Airport Infrastructure Monitoring Platform & NOC

First- and second-line operations are underpinned by an enterprise-grade, open-source-based airport infrastructure monitoring platform deployed at each airport and consolidated at the COC/CCC. It provides unified, real-time visibility across all IT-infrastructure layers, proactive SLA-driven alerting, automated root-cause correlation that reduces Mean Time to Identify, historical performance trending for capacity planning, and webhook integration with the airport ITSM/ticketing platform for automated incident creation — aligned with airport IT governance and ISO/IEC 27001. A distributed proxy is deployed per site with native high-availability clustering.

Platform capabilities:

Multi-layer monitoring of operating systems, hardware (IPMI/SNMP), network, storage and applications in a single pane of glass.

Agent and agentless collection — active/passive agents, SNMP v1/v2c/v3, IPMI, WMI, SSH and HTTP checks.

Automatic network discovery and low-level discovery of interfaces, disks, CPUs, VMs and services.

Expression-based trigger and alerting engine with multi-condition logic, hysteresis and dependency chains to suppress alert storms.

Built-in event correlation and tag-based filtering for rapid triage.

Distributed, proxy-based monitoring for remote sites and DMZ segments with encrypted communications.

Native high-availability cluster with active/standby failover and a shared database backend.

Role-based dashboards, network maps and a NOC video-wall view; ITSM webhook integration (ServiceNow/Jira/Remedy); full audit log with LDAP/AD authentication.

| Infrastructure layer | Representative components | Monitoring method | Priority |
| --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Server infrastructure | Physical servers, VMs, hypervisors | Agent + IPMI / virtualisation API | Critical |
| Network infrastructure | Core/distribution switches, routers, WAN links | SNMP v3 (+ ICMP) | Critical / High |
| Network security | Firewalls, Wi-Fi access points | SNMP v3 + Syslog / REST API | Critical / High |
| Storage systems | SAN / NAS arrays, backup infrastructure | SNMP + REST API / Agent | Critical / High |
| Supporting services | DNS/DHCP/NTP, AD/LDAP, SMTP relay | Agent / WMI / service checks | High / Medium |
| Power & environment | UPS / PDU, temperature & humidity sensors | SNMP v3 / Modbus | Critical / High |

Severity classification and escalation are configured as follows, with dependency-based suppression (e.g. a downed core switch suppresses downstream alarms) and planned-maintenance windows to prevent alert floods:

| Severity | Trigger condition | Initial action | Escalation if no ack | Channel |
| --- | --- | --- | --- | --- |
| Disaster | Core service down / airport-ops impact | Alert on-call engineer + shift lead | IT manager after 5 min | SMS + Email + ITSM P1 |
| High | Performance-critical threshold breached | Alert on-call engineer | Shift lead after 15 min | Email + ITSM P2 |
| Average | Warning threshold exceeded > 10 min | Alert first-line queue | On-call engineer after 30 min | Email + ITSM P3 |
| Warning | Approaching threshold / anomaly | Alert first-line queue / NOC | Re-alert after 1 hr | Email + ITSM P4 |
| Info | Resolved events / discovery changes | Log to ITSM / NOC dashboard | None | ITSM log |

Notification channels include email (SMTP) for all severities, SMS gateway for Disaster/High alerts to on-call engineers, Microsoft Teams/Slack for NOC notifications, automated ITSM ticket creation for Average and above, and a real-time NOC dashboard on the control-room video wall.

## 17.5  LDCS Operation & Airline Onboarding

Beyond supplying the LDCS product (Section 6), RESA–WAISL operates the LDCS as a day-to-day managed service across the eight airports, so that airlines and ground handlers can use the system productively from day one. The operation is delivered by the in-country team under the five-year SLA and is distinct from the LDCS product specification in Section 6.

Operational scope:

Airline onboarding and configuration — setting up DCS profiles, PECTAB/GUI layouts, host links and Type-B/mail connectivity for each airline using the system.

Travel-document and messaging configuration — Timatic / Timatic AutoCheck setup and APIS/APP country configuration, with new API countries added within two weeks.

Weight-and-balance configuration per aircraft type, in coordination with the airline.

Day-to-day operational support to airlines and ground handlers during check-in and boarding, including go-show/rec handling and degraded-mode procedures.

New-airline integration performed jointly with the airline and RESA within agreed timeframes; provider-managed Type-B and mail connectivity.

EAC-customised monthly passenger reporting and operational staffing aligned to the SLA matrix (Section 3.1).

## 17.7  Consumables & Spares Management

RESA–WAISL manages operational consumables and spares for all eight airports across the full five-year term under the comprehensive monthly SLA, ensuring uninterrupted passenger processing. Consumables are replenished proactively per site and included in the monthly SLA at no additional operational charge; usage is tracked and reported.

| Consumable item | Coverage |
| --- | --- |
| ATB / BTP TK180 print heads | Replenished per site across all check-in and boarding-pass / bag-tag printers |
| Epson DCP print heads | Document-printer print heads per site |
| Epson DCP ribbons | Document-printer ribbons replenished on a rolling basis |
| HP laser-printer cartridges | Office/laser printers at every site |
| A4 paper | Standard paper stock for all sites |
| HHT batteries | Replacement batteries for the rugged handheld terminals (BRS / baggage tracking) |

Spares-holding model:

One spare unit per device type is held at each site to maintain continuity (per Section 5 SLA).

Parts unavailable from the manufacturer are replaced within three months, with notification to the EAC repair centre as required.

Consumables and spares are covered by the comprehensive monthly SLA covering servers, devices and all consumables from project start.

## 17.8  Training Delivery Plan

RESA–WAISL delivers a consolidated, end-to-end training programme in English, mapped to the RFP training deliverables and cross-referenced with Section 14. It combines administrator train-the-trainers and knowledge transfer (delivered with RESA, including sessions at RESA premises), local end-user, operations and airline training at each site, and the overseas technical workshop for EAC staff.

Administrator train-the-trainers and knowledge transfer, delivered with RESA, including sessions at RESA premises.

Local end-user, operations and airline training at each airport — 10 EAC trainees per airport (80 total) plus 5 airline trainees per airport, including LDCS and Weight & Balance users.

Overseas technical workshop for 10 EAC staff focused on special airport systems worldwide.

#### Overseas Workshop — Logistics Management

RESA–WAISL manages the full logistics for each overseas training cohort as an integrated service:

Accommodation — sourcing and vetting hotels within budget and near the venue, negotiating group rates, managing bookings and contracts, coordinating check-in/check-out and special requirements, with a 24/7 on-the-ground point of contact.

Travel — booking flights and ground transport, airport and venue transfers, visa applications and invitation letters, travel and medical insurance, and real-time itinerary monitoring with rebooking on disruption.

Training-class management — venue sourcing and AV setup, scheduling with trainers and SMEs, participant registration, attendance tracking and access badges, materials logistics, certification support, and post-programme attendance/feedback reporting.
